findings.title: Integration test lacks platform skip marker
findings.description: TestMitmproxyAfterKeyDeletion.test_mitmproxy_works_after_key_deletion has no @pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform != 'win32', ...) marker. The task spec states this test should be 'skipped on Linux'. If Linux CI runs this test and mitmproxy behaviour differs (e.g. confdir defaults, certutil absence), it could produce flaky failures that mask real issues.
findings.impact: Potential false CI failures on Linux; developers may learn to ignore integration test failures, reducing signal-to-noise ratio in CI.
findings.recommendation: Add skip marker if the intent is Windows-only: @pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform != 'win32', reason='CA deletion integration test targets Windows CI'). Alternatively, if the test is cross-platform, document that explicitly and verify it passes on Linux too.

findings.title: Broad exception swallowing in _shutdown_proxy may hide port leaks
findings.description: _shutdown_proxy swallows all exceptions with bare 'except Exception: pass' blocks. If the proxy server sockets are not properly closed, the OS port remains in TIME_WAIT or LISTEN state. Subsequent test runs reuse get_free_port() which should avoid the same port, but leaked asyncio tasks/threads can accumulate across a full test session.
findings.impact: Test isolation degradation; in theory a leaked proxy could intercept loopback connections from subsequent tests in the same session. Low exploitability in a test context.
findings.recommendation: Wrap _shutdown_proxy in a try/finally that at minimum logs any exception rather than silently discarding it. Consider using asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(master.shutdown_runner(), loop) for a cleaner shutdown path.

notes: The test code correctly verifies the security-critical K-1 fix: private key files are deleted from disk after mitmproxy loads them into memory. No hardcoded secrets, credentials, or injection vectors found. Dummy file content (b'dummy-content', b'key', b'cert') is appropriate for unit tests — no real cryptographic material. The integration test correctly binds to 127.0.0.1 only, preventing any exposure beyond loopback. The production implementation (system_config.py:366-390, main.py:561,577) correctly deletes sensitive files and is covered by the new tests. Overall the implementation reduces the attack surface as intended by K-1.
